data: Large marble plaque, missing the upper left corner and the lower margin.
I.Sicily with the permission of the Assessorato Regionale dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana - Dipartimento dei Beni Culturali e dell’Identità Siciliana; photo J. Prag 1.10.2019

An outstanding collection of archaeological material. The collection ranges from Siracusa and the surrounding territory (Megara Hyblaia, Akrai, Kasmenai etc) to encompass all of eastern Sicily, reaching as far as Gela and Agrigento. The ground floor covers prehistory through to the end of the Classical period. Upstairs includes Hellenistic and Roman Syracuse and a display of material from the catacombs. A gallery displaying material from Hellenistic eastern Sicily is in preparation.
Siracusa. Esplorazioni nelle catacombe di S. Giovanni ed in quelle della vigna Cassia
Paolo Orsi, «Siracusa. Esplorazioni nelle catacombe di S. Giovanni ed in quelle della vigna Cassia», Notizie degli Scavi di Antichità, 1893, 276–314
Museum epigraphicum: seu inscriptionum Christianarum quae in Syracusanis catacumbis repertae sunt corpusculum

V. Strazzulla, Museum epigraphicum: seu inscriptionum Christianarum quae in Syracusanis catacumbis repertae sunt corpusculum, Documenti per servire alla storia di Sicilia : 3. ser. 3 (Palermo: Lo Statuto, 1897)
Le iscrizioni datate della Sicilia paleocristiana
Antonio Ferrua, «Le iscrizioni datate della Sicilia paleocristiana», Kokalos 28–29 (1983 1982): 3–29
C. Wessel, Inscriptiones Graecae Christianae Veteres Occidentis, Inscriptiones Christianae Italiae (Bari: Edipuglia, 1989)

Facsimile Images

Apparatus criticus

  • Text of Wessel, supplemented by transcription of Orsi

English translation

Jonathan Prag

Dionysia and Pompeia(?), her daughter, (lie) here, departed in god, the mother 18 days before the kalends of October [14 September] in the consulship of Mallios Theodoros [399 CE], the daughter 9 days before the kalends of February [24 January] following the fifth consulship of Arkadios and Honorios, the Augusti [402 CE]

commentary

As Ferrua observes, the unusual form of the consular dating for the daughter's death suggests that the notice of the new consuls for 403 CE had not yet reached Syracusae when the epitaph was engraved.

Διονυσία καὶ ❦ Πομπη·ί·α ἡ ταύ της θυγάτηρ ἐνθάδε κῖντε προχωρήσασε ἐν θεῷ ἡ μήτηρ πρὸ ιη καλανδῶν ὀκτοβρίων ὑπατείᾳ Μαλλίῳ Θεοδώρῳ ἡ θυγάτηρ πρὸ · θ · καλανδῶν · φεβρουαρίων · μετὰ τὴν ὑπατίαν Ἀρκαδίου · καὶ · Ὁνω ρίου σσεβαστῶν · τὸ · ε ·
Text of Wessel, supplemented by transcription of Orsi

Dionysia and Pompeia(?), her daughter, (lie) here, departed in god, the mother 18 days before the kalends of October [14 September] in the consulship of Mallios Theodoros [399 CE], the daughter 9 days before the kalends of February [24 January] following the fifth consulship of Arkadios and Honorios, the Augusti [402 CE]

As Ferrua observes, the unusual form of the consular dating for the daughter's death suggests that the notice of the new consuls for 403 CE had not yet reached Syracusae when the epitaph was engraved.
